RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-[1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium-2-yl]ethenyl]-4-nitrophenol, also referred to as Compound 8, is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-hydroxy-5-nitrostyryl group at C2 and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl moiety at C4 of the quinoline ring that is synthesized via general procedure B, and this compound is a mono-cationic quinolinium dye featuring a permanently charged 1-methylquinolin-1-ium core, linked via an ethenyl bridge to a phenyl ring substituted with a strong nitro group and a phenolic hydroxyl group, the electron-withdrawing nitro group and the pH-sensitive hydroxyl group extend the conjugation system, making it a highly efficient and pH-sensitive fluorophore, in practice, it is typically designed as a pH-responsive cellular probe to monitor the acidity within living cells or specific organelles, such as lysosomes, its cationic nature also suggests potential for DNA binding and mitochondrial accumulation, allowing for its use as an antimicrobial or anticancer agent, and it binds ADAM10 mRNAs QGRS with moderate affinity but shows weak translational up-regulation, unlike potent analogs, its nitro-substituted styryl group limits optimal RNA G-quadruplex interactions, failing to robustly enhance ADAM10 expression or reduce A production.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 15, is a highly substituted chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, and Compound 15 was identified as a top lead compound with very high affinity and exceptional selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, the chromene moiety is utilized in antiviral and antitumor drug design, the presence of a bromo atom, amino group, and benzoyl group suggests it may act as a potent enzyme inhibitor or receptor antagonist, potentially blocking key processes in cancer cell proliferation, its bromine substituent enhanced RNA binding through additional halogen bonding, it significantly reduced polyQ aggregates, was cell-permeable, and showed low toxicity. RNA

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-2-[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoylamino]acetamide, also referred to as Compound H9 (a bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258), is a molecule with a linear acetamide backbone and a central urea group, with both ends substituted by a phenyl ring bearing a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) group, the imidazolinyl group is strongly basic and is almost fully protonated at physiological pH, making this compound a classic example of a polycationic molecule, the central urea (N(H)CON(H)) and amide (CON(H)) linkages provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, due to its length, rigidity, and strong cationic nature, this molecule is designed as a nucleic acid binding agent, intended to interact tightly with the phosphate backbone of DNA or RNA via electrostatic forces, and possibly through intercalation between base pairs, such compounds are frequently investigated as broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents (especially antifungal and antiprotozoal) or as gene expression modulators, while Compound H9 retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic p-chlorophenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)exp RNA (the pathogenic RNA causing myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)) with greater potency than Hoechst 33258 though it is less selective than H1 as it binds with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, and in DM1 cell models it can partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal while showing cytotoxicity at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 8-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-8,9-dihydro-7H-pyrano[3,2-e]benzimidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 12, this compound features a benzimidazole scaffold and two dimethylamino side chains, a structure similar to many drugs with anticancer, antifungal, and antiviral activities, and it is a conformationally locked analog of the 2-aminobenzimidazole class that was purposely designed to bind and modulate the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES) subdomain IIa, a phylogenetically conserved RNA element that governs cap-independent viral translation; in this derivative the flexible C6 side-chain of the parent scaffold is cyclized into a six-membered tetrahydropyran ring, which brings about significant affinity gain over its open-chain predecessor while retaining negligible cytotoxicity to human hepatoma cells; crystallographic and FRET analyses show that the ligand slips into the IIa internal bulge where its two protonatable dimethylamino groups replace two divalent-metal hot spots and its planar heteroaromatic face -stacks with G5, forcing the native inter-helical bend to widen, which further mis-orients the downstream IIb hairpin, blocks eIF2-GDP release and stalls 80-S ribosome assembly; in relevant antiviral activity assays it suppresses viral RNA and infectious particle production with notable potency that tracks quantitatively with its RNA-binding constant and is retained against common clinical IIa mutants, and its favorable in-vitro window, synthetic accessibility and structure-verified mode of action make it a validated lead for the structure-guided design of next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals against hepatitis C, in addition, benzimidazole derivatives are widely studied as potential new anti-tumor lead compounds due to their favorable interaction with biomacromolecules such as DNA and topoisomerases.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: 5′UAG/3′A_C (SL I): The UAG/A_C motif is located within HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I. Identified in the Inforna analysis as a ligandable element, it was enriched in binding assays, indicating its potential as a structural determinant for small molecule recognition and functional viral RNA inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: AU is a fully paired RNA . It shows no binding to compound 2 . Used as a control in affinity/selectivity assays for 2, highlighting 2’s specificity for r(CUG)₁₂ over fully paired RNAs to support its role targeting r(CUG)exp in DM1.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. Small_molecule_information: N-[(E)-[10-[(E)-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]anthracen-9-yl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Bisantrene, is a complex anthracene derivative with known antitumor relevance, where the anthracene ring (three linearly fused benzene rings) is substituted at the 9- and 10-positions with hydrazone/imine-linked 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups; anthracene is a well-known fluorescent chromophore, making this compound highly likely to exhibit strong fluorescence, and the molecule contains two imidazolinium groups and a conjugated hydrazone-imine structure, elements that facilitate binding to biomolecules like DNA, consistent with its use as a DNA-intercalating topoisomerase IIrelated agent, and such compounds are typically designed as DNA-binding fluorescent probes or potential anticancer agents

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the derivative with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline scaffold that bears a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, integrates the propylamine spacer with the 4-methylpiperazine ring, and this compound showed strong binding to the NRQ as well as potent anti-proliferative activity in several cell lines while being less active in some other cell lines; this piperazine-propylamine side chain is found in various CNS-acting agents like antipsychotics and antidepressants, suggesting an ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and modulate G-protein coupled receptors, and the compound is a structural candidate for anticancer, antifungal, or CNS-active drugs, leveraging the high basicity and lipophilicity of the side chain to enhance bioavailability and tissue distribution.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[11-[2-(dimethylamino)ethylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

Small_molecule_information: 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-one, also referred to as CP11, is an isocoumarin derivative, and CP11 is a chromanone (dihydrobenzopyranone) derivative with a phenyl ring whose saturated chromone core exhibited a single, modest-affinity binding mode, lacking the desired specificity for the target RNA structure, the isocoumarin scaffold, fused from a benzene ring and a six-membered lactone ring (dihydro-pyranone), is prevalent in natural products, especially fungal and plant metabolites, these naturally occurring isocoumarin derivatives often exhibit diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, 3-phenyl-3,4-dihydroisochromen-1-ones structure includes a phenyl group at the 3-position of the lactone ring, which increases the molecules lipophilicity and steric complexity, in reality, compounds with this structure serve as basic building blocks for the synthesis of various natural product analogues and pharmaceutical precursors, where researchers explore and optimize their potential pharmacological activity through further chemical modifications (e.g., introducing other functional groups on the phenyl or isocoumarin rings). RNA information: miR-1587 is a G-rich microRNA that forms a stable parallel G-quadruplex structure, confirmed by biophysical methods. It directly targets and downregulates TAGLN, an important tumor suppressor and marker of smooth muscle differentiation, at both mRNA and protein levels. Its function is structurally modulated: KCl-induced G-quadruplex formation inhibits its activity by blocking seed region access. Ligands further regulate its function—pseudopalmatine stabilizes the quadruplex and enhances suppression, while TMPyP4 disrupts the structure and restores miRNA-mediated repression.
Small_molecule_information: 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is a tetracationic porphyrin-like compound where the dihydroporphyrin (chlorin) core is substituted at the four meso positions (5, 10, 15, 20) with four 1-methylpyridinium groups, with the formula C44H38N8 (excluding counterions); this compound is also a cationic porphyrin known as a G-quadruplex ligand that acts as a destabilizer of the miR-1587 G-quadruplex, which can disrupt and prevent the formation of the stable parallel G-quadruplex structure induced by potassium ions (as evidenced by a concentration-dependent decrease in the characteristic CD spectral signal of the quadruplex), and by binding to and unfolding the secondary structure, it frees the seed region of miR-1587 to make it available for interaction with its complementary target mRNA sequence, thereby enhancing the functional activity of miR-1587, leading to increased binding to the TAGLN 3UTR and stronger repression of the reporter gene, while its effect is negligible on a mutated target plasmid, confirming that its function is specifically tied to enabling miRNA-mRNA binding rather than direct interference with the translation machinery; in addition, these tetracationic porphyrin derivatives are prominent photosensitizers, and the chlorin core enables highly efficient absorption of visible light and subsequent generation of reactive oxygen species (like singlet oxygen, 1O2), with its real-world application lying in Photodynamic Therapy (PDT), where it is designed to selectively accumulate in tumor tissues and, upon light activation, specifically kill cancer cells through oxidative stress.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a typical dicationic structure, consisting of the azonium on the pentacyclic scaffold and the azonium on the 3-methylpyridinium ring. This combination of dual positive charges and a highly lipophilic large scaffold makes it a potent cell membrane permeabilizer. Pyridinium rings are common cationic units. In practical applications, such amphiphilic, highly charged compounds are widely explored for disrupting the integrity of biological membranes, thus exhibiting potent, broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against both bacteria and fungi. It may also be studied as a cellular tracer or fluorescent probe for bioimaging.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-2-yl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzamide, also referred to as Compound 18 which is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative and a regioisomer with a 2-benzoic amide moiety, is a positional isomer where the benzamide group is attached to the 2-amino group of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine ring, leaving the 3-position with a free amino group, and it also carries the 3-(trifluoromethyl)benzoyl group; this compound binds HIV-1 TAR and RRE RNAs, key elements for viral replication, and as a potential RNA-binding ligand, it targets HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, though with moderate affinity compared to other analogs, meanwhile the study of such amide-position isomers is crucial in drug discovery, as small changes in linkage position can lead to significant differences in biological activity, metabolic stability, and toxicity, and this molecule is also categorized as a candidate for a kinase inhibitor, researched for its potential anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory effects.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as C43, is a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, which is based on the coumarin core, with the C7 position linked to the quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group, the C3 substituent is the 8-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl group, a heterocycle formed by the fusion of an imidazole ring and a pyrazine ring, with a methyl group at the C8 position of the pyrazine ring, and it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, while imidazopyrazine structures are key components of many kinase inhibitors and anti-inflammatory drugs, and combining this pharmacophore with the fluorescent coumarin core, this compound is likely designed to target specific kinases, utilizing its fluorescence for high-throughput screening, or as a potential anti-inflammatory or anti-proliferative agent.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-1 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-2 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-3 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-4 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-5 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 8-cycloheptyl-1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as CP5 which contains a cycloheptane ring connected to a complex imidazolinone and dimethylurea heterocyclic system, is a xanthine derivative similar to caffeine; CP5 has a bulky, multi-heterocyclic structure that showed binding but was likely hampered by poor specificity, preventing its selection for detailed study, while this compound may have bronchodilator, myocardial stimulant, or central nervous system modulatory activity, and is often explored in drug development as a new phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: quinazoline-2,4,5,6-tetramine, also referred to as 2,4,5,6-Tetraaminoquinoxaline, is a simple polyamino derivative based on the fundamental quinazoline heterocyclic core, substituted with four amino groups at positions 2, 4, 5, and 6. Quinazolines are a prominent scaffold in medicinal chemistry known for diverse biological activities. Due to the multiple electron-donating amino groups, the compound is highly electron-rich and basic, and it is also a low-molecular-weight heteroaromatic base that mimics arginine-rich peptides by lodging into the HIV-1 TAR RNA three-nucleotide bulge, with its planar bicyclic quinoxaline core stacking between conserved purines while the four exocyclic amino groups donate a precisely positioned network of hydrogen bonds that closely match the electrostatic signature required for Tat recognition; consequently, the compound competitively blocks Tat binding, abolishes Tat-activated transcription in HeLa reporter cells, and suppresses viral p24 production in latently infected OM-10.1 cells without accelerating the off-rate of pre-bound Tat, and its small size, high chemical stability, and preferential affinity for the bulge over double-stranded DNA or tRNA make it an attractive scaffold for further optimization toward orally available, transcription-targeted anti-HIV therapeutics. Research on polyamino quinazolines often focuses on their potential as enzyme inhibitors, particularly kinase inhibitors, as the quinazoline core is found in many successful anti-cancer kinase drugs (e.g., gefitinib). Furthermore, its structure may also be explored for antimalarial or immunomodulatory activities.

RNA information: ATP-40-1 is an ATP-binding RNA aptamer with distinct structure-function traits. Structurally, it contains a conserved 11-nucleotide motif and forms a stable stem-loop secondary structure—both are critical for maintaining its active conformation. Functionally, it binds ATP specifically. It also binds AMP and adenosine but shows no affinity for GTP, UTP, CTP, or ITP. As a key model, it aids in elucidating RNA-ligand interaction mechanisms by linking structural conservation to specific, moderate-affinity binding.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3R,4S,5S)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-purin-9-yloxolan-2-yl]dihydrogenphosphate, also referred to as Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP), with the chemical structure C10H13N5O7P, is a typical nucleotide molecule. The compound is composed of a purine nucleobase (specifically 1 molecule of adenine), a ribose (oxolan ring), and 1 molecule of phosphate group, linked via the characteristic N-glycosidic bond and phosphate ester bond (additionally connected by a phosphodiester bond), bearing high stereochemical similarity to natural nucleotides like Guanosine Monophosphate (GMP); compared with ATP, it lacks 2 phosphate groups and has no high-energy phosphate bonds, making it one of the basic units of RNA. Nucleotides are the fundamental building blocks of all living organisms, participating in essential life processes such as energy storage (ATP), nucleic acid synthesis (DNA/RNA), and cell signaling (cAMP/cGMP), and for this compound specifically, functionally it mainly serves as a precursor for nucleotide synthesis: it can be phosphorylated to form ADP (adenosine diphosphate), which is further converted into ATP, participating in the substrate cycle of cellular energy metabolism, meanwhile it is an important component of RNA, involved in the transmission and expression of genetic information, and like ATP and adenosine, it can effectively elute specifically bound RNAs from ATP-agarose columns, demonstrating that its adenosine moiety (the core structure shared with ATP) is key for RNA binding, while the number of phosphate groups has little impact on this binding process; thus, if this compound is the natural nucleotide itself, it performs its endogenous physiological functions within the cell; if it is a synthetic analog, it would be designed as an inhibitor or substrate for purine metabolism enzymes or reverse transcriptase, primarily used for antiviral or anti-cancer therapy by being incorporated into nucleic acid chains to cause chain termination.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[15-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-6-oxa-11,13,17-triazatetracyclo[8.7.0.02,7.012,17]heptadeca-1(10),2(7),8,11-tetraen-4-yl]-N,N-dimethylmethanamine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 13 (Isis-13), is a complex polycyclic macrocyclic structure with multiple heteroatoms, and Benzimidazole 13 (Isis-13) is a bicyclic small molecule purposely optimized to recognize and lock the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES) subdomain IIa, an RNA stem-bulge element that must maintain its native 90 bend for efficient cap-independent viral translation, built on a 2-aminobenzimidazole core, Isis-13 simultaneously constrains both the N1 and C6 side-chains into cyclic motifsN1 into a pyrrolidine and C6 into a tetrahydropyranyielding a significant affinity gain over the original hit while remaining non-toxic to human hepatoma cells, due to its unique scaffold, it is a potential candidate for novel antimicrobial agents or receptor antagonists, mimicking biological active sites, in the binding pocket the two protonatable dimethylamino groups replace two divalent-metal hot spots detected in crystal structures; the planar benzimidazole ring -stacks with G5, forcing the bulge to straighten by 23 and thereby mis-align the downstream IIb hairpin, this conformational switch blocks eIF2-GDP release, stalls 80-S ribosome assembly and suppresses HCV replication in genotype-1b replicon and infectious JFH-1 luciferase-reporter assays with notable potency, a potency that tracks quantitatively with its RNA-binding constant and is retained against common clinical IIa mutants, the favorable in-vitro window, confirmed mechanism of action and synthetic scalability identify Isis-13 as a validated lead for structure-guided development of next-generation RNA-directed antivirals against hepatitis C.

RNA information: The 5 '-UTR of Bacillus subtilis tenA contains a classic TPP ribose switch: the secondary structure predicted by its mfold is composed of P1-P5 stem rings forming TPP binding pockets, which was confirmed by in-line probing in vitro; When the concentration of TPP (or toxic analogue PTPP) in cells increases, the ligand binds to the ribose switch with high affinity, inducing the expression platform to fold into a transcription terminator, thereby closing the transcription of the entire tenA-tenI-goxB-thiS-thiG-thiF-yjbV operator. The proteins encoded by these genes are responsible for the synthesis and salvage pathways of thiamine precursors HMP-PP and Thz-P. Therefore, ribose switches maintain TPP homeostasis through negative feedback. Mutations such as G94A in PT-resistant strains weaken the stability of P1 stems, causing the ribose switch to lose its ligand-sensing ability and the operon to be continuously expressed, thereby revealing that the TPP ribose switch is the target of the antibacterial compound pyrithiamine.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-[(4-amino-2-methylpyrimidin-5-yl)methyl]-4-methyl-1,3-thiazol-3-ium-5-yl]ethyl phosphono hydrogen phosphate chloride, also referred to as Thiamine Pyrophosphate (TPP) or Cocarboxylase, is a phosphate ester salt containing pyrimidine and thiazolium structures that is formed by the condensation of two precursors, hydroxymethylpyrimidine (HMP) and thiazole (Thz), after pyrophosphorylation; its structure is based on the pyrimidine and thiazole parent skeletons: the pyrimidine ring is substituted at position 5 with a (thiazoliumyl)methyl group, and the thiazolium ring is substituted at position 5 with a phosphorylated ethanol group, and in terms of biological function, this compound is the IUPAC name for the active form of Vitamin B1, which is a vital coenzyme and cofactor that catalyzes glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and the cleavage of C-C bonds in the pentose phosphate pathway as well as numerous enzymatic reactions including those in cellular energy production (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase complex), and its concentration is sensed in real time by the TPP riboswitch on mRNA - at high concentrations, it triggers transcription termination or translation masking, turning off all genes related to synthesis, transport and recycling; at low concentrations, the inhibition is released, thereby maintaining TPP homeostasis in bacteria and fungi through negative feedback and becoming a target for the antibacterial drug pyrithiamine.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propan-1-amine This molecule features a 3-aminopropoxy chain connected via an ether linkage. This structure, containing a primary amine, also functions as a dicationic species (quaternary core plus protonated primary amine) under physiological conditions. Compounds with the propylamine backbone are often studied as Histamine H2 receptor antagonists or antidepressants. Its structure gives it the potential for strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cellular components (like DNA) or the phospholipid membrane, which may confer antimicrobial activity or utility as a gene transfection aid.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(7-aza-2-azoniaspiro[3.5]nonan-7-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM19, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is the 7-aza-2-azoniaspiro[3.5]nonan-7-yl group, bearing a 1 charge, with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system; this is a double-nitrogen spirocyclic structure, created by the fusion of a four-membered ring and a six-membered ring (like piperidine) sharing one common atom, the quaternary ammonium positive charge is located on the six-membered ring, enhancing water solubility and electrostatic affinity to negatively charged targets, and this spirocyclic structure confers high rigidity and a specific three-dimensional conformation, crucial for achieving greater geometric selectivity in target binding, which targets the single-G bulge of RNA via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, offering moderate selectivity for bulged-G RNA, and the combination of the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and the coumarin core suggests potential as a highly selective ligand for targeted receptors or kinases, possibly targeting receptors associated with neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimers or Parkinsons.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[2-(dimethylamino)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(5-amino-4-carbamoylimidazol-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as 5-Aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide ribonucleotide (AICAR), is a non-azaaromatic purine nucleotide intermediate with an imidazole ring (bearing amino and carboxamide groups), a ribose moiety, and a phosphate group that lacks planar azaaromatic features, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; the molecule is a vital cellular metabolite and signaling molecule, a key intermediate in the de novo purine biosynthesis pathway (wherein its core role lies, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), and its phosphorylated form ZMP is a potent activator of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which is a crucial enzyme regulating cellular energy balance, making the molecule important in the study of metabolic diseases (such as type 2 diabetes) and energy metabolism.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of a 3-styryl substituted benzo[furo[3,2-b]quinoline with a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain, is the most complex quaternary ammonium salt in this series, featuring the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core and an N-methylpiperazine side chain, it binds to the NRQ, exhibits potent anti-proliferative activity particularly in Huh7 cells, was one of the compounds identified in the lower left quadrant of the binding vs. activity plot though it had poor selectivity, and is a highly optimized candidate for anticancer research, aiming for maximum DNA-intercalating potency and favorable pharmaceutical properties.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: diethyl-[2-[(2-thiophen-2-ylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)amino]ethyl]azanium, also referred to as a thienylquinoline derivative, is a dicationic quinoline derivative with a thiophene ring that exhibits weak RNA binding and low specificity, has limited antiviral potency and may act via non-specific mechanisms; such poly-cationic heterocycles are studied as potential antiparasitic drugs or as fluorescence imaging agents due to their DNA binding characteristics.

RNA information: It serves as an in vitro research tool to detect the binding properties of small molecules to composite RNA motifs: the monomeric Compound 1 has weak binding affinity , while the dimeric Compound 2 shows significantly enhanced affinity for it. Such studies provide key data support for clarifying the selective binding mechanism of Compound 2 to pri-miR-515.; RNA 1 (Drosha 885) is an RNA fragment designed to mimic the Drosha processing site in pri-miR-885, with its core structural feature being the presence of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-885. It is used to detect the binding properties between small molecules and RNA. Such interaction studies provide key data support for screening selective small molecules that target specific miRNA precursors.

cell regulation.

Small_molecule_information: Quinoline, also referred to as an azaaromatic compound with a planar hydrophobic benzo[b]pyridine scaffold that interacts non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA and shows minimal activation of riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, is one of the most important nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocycles formed by the fusion of a benzene ring with a pyridine ring with the molecular formula, and it is a colorless hygroscopic liquid with a strong odor whose skeleton forms the core of many natural products such as cinchona alkaloids and synthetic drugs such as chloroquine and quinolone antibiotics, with quinoline derivatives widely used in the synthesis of dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals and exhibiting diverse biological activities including antibacterial, antimalarial, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer effects.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 6 which is a triazole analog with altered N-methylation, is a complex, linear peptidic analog that has a relatively strong affinity for HIV-1 FSS RNA with reduced selectivity under tRNA competition, alters the Gag/Gag-Pol ratio, suppresses pseudotyped HIV infectivity to show strong antiviral activity, and displays a certain level of cytotoxicity that makes it more toxic than compound 5 despite their chemical similarity, highlighting how subtle structural changes in the triazole scaffold can shift the balance between potency and safety; its structure features two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments, linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, and similar to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide and N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, this compound incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline moiety, and the two repeating units are slightly different: one unit has a methylcarbamoyl substitution on the phenylalanine, while the other has a carbamoyl substitution, the molecule contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]ethanamine (also referred to as the target compound) was synthesized via palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); functionally, this molecule can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, though a high ligand/Bh molar ratio is required to produce detectable spectral changes and its binding affinity is relatively low, and it is a complex molecule containing multiple primary amine groups (RNH2) and a polyphenol ether structure, whose scaffold is composed of two substituted phenyl rings linked by an ether oxygen, with a total of four 2-aminoethyl side chains and one methoxy group on the rings, and this polyamine structure grants the molecule high cationic character and hydrophilicity at physiological pH, enabling strong interaction with anionic biomolecules, while in practice, polyamine-like molecules are often used as non-viral gene delivery vectors, forming stable complexes with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) to facilitate transfection, or as modulators of polyamine metabolic pathways, and its high cationic nature also suggests potential antimicrobial or antifungal activity.

Small_molecule_information: 3-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]propanoic acidThis compound links the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with a Histidine residue via a triglycine linker. The potential activity is antineoplastic, utilizing the imidazole ring of Histidine for interaction. In reality, the inclusion of Histidine often targets the acidic tumor microenvironment, as the side chain becomes protonated in acidic conditions, which can enhance cellular uptake or specific binding within tumor tissue.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR(WT) RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-1-methyl-4-oxo-7-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)quinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as WM5 (1-methyl-7-[4-(2-pyridyl)-1-piperazinyl]-6-amino-4-oxo-1,4-dihydroquinoline-3-carboxylic acid), this compound is a structural analog of quinolone antibiotics (like Ofloxacin), originally optimized as a quinolone antibacterial but subsequently found to act as a high-affinity ligand for the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulge, it retains the canonical 4-oxo-3-carboxylate warhead, replaces the customary C-6 fluorine with an unsubstituted amino group and carries a small N-1 methyl and a bulky 4-(2-pyridyl)-1-piperazine at C-7, can bind TAR RNA, block Tat-activated transcription, suppress HIV-1 replication in both acutely and chronically infected T-cell lines without affecting reverse transcriptase, integrase or protease activities, while esterification of its carboxylate (WM5E) abolishes both RNA binding and antiviral efficacy, the compound displays low micromolar cytotoxicity and is essentially inactive against HSV-1, underscoring its selectivity for HIV-1 and establishing it as a promising lead for a new class of transcription-targeted antiretrovirals that act by high-affinity recognition of viral RNA rather than classical hosttopoisomerase inhibition, and is likely a quinolone derivative designed to explore new antibacterial or antiproliferative activity.

Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-2-[[2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]hexanoic acid is the benzofuroquinoline core conjugated via the glycine-glycine-lysine (Gly-Gly-Lys) tripeptide. The Gly-Gly sequence often serves as an enzyme cleavable linker, while lysine provides alkaline targeting. This structure is used in prodrug design, aiming for tumor-specific drug release by enzymatic hydrolysis in the tumor microenvironment to achieve targeted delivery and reduce systemic toxicity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylimidazol-3-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a molecule with a dicationic character, consisting of the core cationic pentacyclic scaffold linked via a propoxy bridge to a 1-methylimidazolium ring. Imidazolium is a common quaternary ammonium structure. The presence of two positive charges significantly enhances the molecules polarity and lipophilicity/amphiphilic dual characteristics. This structural motif is often designed in medicinal chemistry as a cell membrane permeabilizer or nucleic acid transfection agent. In practical applications, dicationic structures are frequently explored as novel antifungal or antibacterial agents due to their strong surface activity and membrane-disrupting capabilities. The imidazolium unit may also confer potential as an ionic liquid or phase-transfer catalyst.
