Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the key hit compound with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core, the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position, and a 2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, features the short ethylamine-4-methylpiperazine side chain; this compound displayed the strongest binding affinity among all relevant compounds and potent anti-proliferative activity across all cancer cell lines, though it did not repress NRAS translation like 4a-10, suggesting a different mechanism or binding mode, and its short-chain piperazine is a classic motif in highly potent DNA intercalators and topoisomerase inhibitors, with the short ethylamine spacer being crucial for establishing the optimal electrostatic interaction distance between the positively charged terminus and the DNA phosphate backbone, making it an important structure for highly affine nucleic acid-targeting anticancer drug development.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 6-pyrrolidin-1-yl-2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine, also referred to as 3b, is built upon the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core, with a pyrrolidin-1-yl or piperidine group directly attached to the 6-position. The direct attachment of the nitrogen atom allows the ring to influence the electronic properties and basicity of the pyridine system more directly compared to a methylene-linked structure. This class of nitrogen-containing heterocyclic amines is widely studied as scaffolds for kinase inhibitors or GPCR ligands. The ring moiety enhances lipophilicity, which aids in cell penetration and bioavailability, and this compound exhibits good affinity for pre-miR-21 with preferential binding over duplex DNA or tRNA, although it lacks Dicer-mediated pre-miR-21 cleavage inhibition, serving to elucidate structure-activity relationships for miR-21 maturation inhibitors and representing an early-stage research molecule potentially for antitumor, anti-inflammatory, or nervous system applications.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethylamino)phenol, also referred to as 4o, is a small-molecule heterocyclic amine compound with the molecular formula C14H15N3O, and 4o is a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo-[3,2]-pyridine derivative with a fluorine-substituted aniline moiety, synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition and nucleophilic substitution (sustainable, modular route), the molecule contains an o-aminophenol structure, where the adjacent hydroxyl and amine groups can serve as chelating sites for metal ions and impart potential antioxidant properties to the molecule, it is connected via a methylene secondary amine to a 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine heterocyclic scaffold, a fused heterocyclic structure found in many potentially biologically active molecules, this secondary amine linker provides some molecular flexibility, due to its aminophenol moiety, the compound may act as an inhibitor of metalloenzymes, exerting its pharmacological effect by chelating metal ions like zinc or iron in the active site, its potential activity ranges across anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, or kinase inhibition, 4o exhibits weak affinity for pre-miR-21, far lower than analogs with unsubstituted or CF3-aniline groups, it lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity, showing poor selectivity and potency, likely due to its aniline substituent failing to form critical H-bonds or -stacking with pre-miR-21. pre-miR-21

RNA information: The r(CCUG)₁₀ sequence models the pathogenic RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 2. Structurally, it contains four-nucleotide repeats that also produce hairpins with mismatches, although their geometry differs slightly from CUG repeats. These motifs create similar alternating stripes of electrostatic potential, making selectivity between CUG and CCUG challenging for both proteins and small molecules. Functionally, r(CCUG)₁₀ sequesters MBNL1 in a manner comparable to CUG repeats, contributing to splicing defects. Its inclusion in binding studies demonstrates the difficulty of achieving discrimination between closely related repeat RNAs and emphasizes the need for ligands that exploit subtle structural differences.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2R,11R)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 11 (2012), is a giant, symmetrical peptidomimetic dimer that is closely related to compound 10 but differs in linker geometry, producing subtle variations in conformational dynamics which influences how the benzo[g]quinoline elements align with repeat motifs and contributes to a broader interaction profile, and structurally this highlights the importance of optimizing linker design to balance stability, affinity, and selectivity while functionally it shows significant bioactivity not only in cell assays but also in a DM1 mouse model, where it partially restores proper splicing patterns, making it a key proof-of-concept that synthetic small molecules can achieve therapeutic rescue of RNA-mediated disease in vivo; its backbone is a twelve-carbon chain containing a trans (E) double bond, giving it a relatively extended geometric conformation, and the molecule possesses significant polycationic character due to the presence of multiple amine groups, including the 6-amino groups of two Lysine side chains and two 3-aminopropylamine termini, which polycationic nature is key to designing highly efficient DNA minor groove binders, as it enables potent electrostatic binding to the negatively charged DNA phosphate backbone, furthermore, benzo[g]quinoline groups are attached to both sides, providing DNA intercalation potential or acting as fluorescent reporters, and this design, integrating dual heads, polycationicity, and DNA intercalation units, is a strategy for creating a dual-mechanism antitumor drug, likely acting via topoisomerase inhibition and/or DNA damage.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-indol-3-yl)propanoic acidThis conjugate combines the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with Arginine and Tryptophan. Tryptophan, with its indole ring, is the most hydrophobic and strongly fluorescent amino acid. Featuring two strong fluorophores, this compound is highly likely to be designed as a high-sensitivity dual-fluorescence probe for live-cell imaging and molecular interaction studies. Its potential activity involves functioning as a cytotoxic drug precursor while allowing for real-time tracking of its distribution and target binding.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoic acidA simpler benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline conjugate with a short peptide chain consisting of three Glycine units and one Arginine residue. The potential activity is anticancer driven by the cores mechanism (DNA intercalation/Topoisomerase inhibition) and the Arginines affinity for nucleic acids. This structure is often utilized in drug optimization to determine the minimal peptide length required for retaining biological activity and desired pharmacokinetic properties.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1H-quinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 3 (a class of 2-phenylquinolone derivatives), differs from 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one in that the N-methyl on the quinolinone ring is replaced by a hydrogen atom (1H-quinolin-4-one)N1R H5,7-R OMeR OMeX On 3R -N-N-HDNATat-TARN1

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2-[[4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid is the chemical structure for Folic Acid (FA), also known as Vitamin B9 and also referred to as a non-azaaromatic B-vitamin (B9), which is structured with an oxidized pteridine ring, para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), and a glutamic acid moietyunlike its reduced form tetrahydrofolate (THF), and it lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; folic acid is a vital B vitamin required for cell division, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell maturation, it must be biologically activated by reduction to tetrahydrofolate (THF) by the enzyme DHFR, with its core role being a dietary precursor that needs to be converted to THF to act as a one-carbon carrier for nucleotide synthesis and cell division, which is critical for fetal development and red blood cell production; clinically, the compound is widely used as a nutritional supplement, especially for pregnant women to prevent fetal neural tube defects, and for treating megaloblastic anemia caused by folate deficiency, and many anticancer drugs (e.g., methotrexate) are competitive inhibitors of this molecules activation pathway.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-chloro-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 11, a chlorinated derivative with a methylbenzoyl group that showed promising RNA-binding properties and was used in comparative studies with other halogenated analogs, shares the complex chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core, its substituents include a 3-amino group, a 4-phenyl group, and a 2-4-methylbenzoyl group, but it is specifically substituted with a chlorine atom at the 9-position, the 9-chloro substitution is a common lipophilicity modifier in medicinal chemistry, significantly increasing lipophilicity, improving membrane permeability, and potentially engaging in halogen bond interactions with the target, this polycyclic structure is typically researched as a candidate for a kinase inhibitor or signaling pathway modulator, with potential anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory activity, and it represents a key molecule in Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) studies, where substituents are varied to optimize pharmacological properties.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-2-[[11-[3-(dimethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]acetamide iodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-N-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic compound based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium core with diiodide ions. Its dicationic character comes from the core N-methylation and the 1-methylpiperidin-1-ium quaternary ammonium group at position 7. This compound is investigated for targeted therapy and as a bio-probe. In practical terms, the structure is designed to optimize its binding selectivity toward specific DNA sequences or G-quadruplex (G4) structures, which is crucial for developing G4 stabilizer anti-cancer drugs that specifically regulate oncogene expression.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-fluoro-2-methylindazol-6-yl)-5-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazole, also referred to as SMSM7, is a unique cationic heterocyclic compound, with SMSM7 being a non-planar coumarin derivative that has a 4-ethyl-3-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system and bears a 1 charge, which binds the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, validated by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand scaffold; its (2-(4-fluoro-2-methylindazol-6-yl)-5-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazole) scaffold is the thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazole system, which is commonly found in kinase inhibitors, at position 5, it is substituted with a protonated (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group, this quaternary salt structure ensures the molecule is permanently cationic, increasing its water solubility and electrostatic interaction with anionic biological components, the indazole ring at position 2 is substituted with a fluorine atom and a methyl group, given the combination of thiazole and indazole units, which are prevalent in drug design, this compound may be investigated as a potential kinase inhibitor for anti-cancer or anti-inflammatory drug development, aiming to block abnormal signaling by binding to the kinase ATP pocket.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 9 (2012), is a highly complex asymmetric peptidomimetic bearing the benzo[g]quinoline moiety; while benzo[g]quinoline is a necessary aromatic element for relevant interactions, DCC 9 (2012) has an altered peptide framework that disrupts the orientation required for strong and selective recognition, resulting in a weaker interaction profile, limited ability to discriminate among target sequences, and minimal biological effects, which confirms that benzo[g]quinoline is necessary but not sufficient and that correct positioning within the molecular scaffold is equally critical, highlighting its role as a negative control and supporting evidence for the structureactivity relationship developed across the compound series. The molecules core eight-carbon chain contains a trans (E) double bond, and its most significant feature is the unequal length of the polyamine chains attached to the two termini: one is a 3-aminopropylamine (three carbons) and the other is a 2-aminoethylamine (two carbons); this asymmetric polyamine structure is designed to potentially cause differences in binding affinity or mode with biological targets, and the molecule is symmetrically linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine derivatives on both sides, integrating DNA intercalation capability with polycationic electrostatic binding, with this design combining asymmetry and dual-targeting groups aiming to investigate how regulating asymmetric conformation can enhance the selectivity and multi-action efficacy against specific biological targets, holding potential application in multi-target anti-cancer therapy.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 6-[3-(dimethylamino)propoxy]-1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]benzimidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 3, is a compound that has a benzimidazole core with two dimethylaminoalkoxy/alkyl side chains, and Benzimidazole 3 is a small-molecule ligand engineered to selectively recognize and bind the subdomain IIa of the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES), an RNA structural element essential for cap-independent viral translation, such structures are often explored in medicinal chemistry for their antifungal, antiviral, or antiprotozoal activity, the benzimidazole motif is similar to natural purines, allowing it to interfere with microbial nucleic acid metabolism, making it a potential antiparasitic or antimicrobial drug, structurally, it is based on a 2-aminobenzimidazole core that is substituted at the N1 and C6 positions with flexible cationic side-chains; these protonatable amines mimic two divalent-metal hot spots detected in the native RNA bulge, allowing Benzimidazole 3 to dock into the conserved internal loop with notable affinity and 10-fold selectivity over non-target RNAs, upon binding, the ligand displaces key purine bases (A6, A7) that normally stack within the bulge, straightening the 90 inter-helical bend characteristic of free subdomain IIa; this conformational switch mis-aligns the downstream IIb hairpin, preventing its correct positioning on the 40S ribosomal E-site and thereby blocking eIF2-GDP release and 80S ribosome assembly, in HCV replicon assays Benzimidazole 3 reduces viral RNA levels with notable potency while exhibiting minimal cytotoxicity, and its potency is maintained in infectious JFH-1 luciferase-reporter systems, because it acts upstream of all protein-coding genes, Benzimidazole 3 offers a conserved, genotype-independent target and provides a validated scaffold for structure-guided optimization toward novel direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C.

RNA information: BS-yxjA is a guanine riboswitch derived from Bacillus subtilis. Its structure consists of a highly conserved aptamer region, which is capable of specifically recognizing and binding to guanine. This aptamer region contains three stem-ring structures (P1, P2 and P3), which are organized around a three-way junction point to form a guanine binding site. The function of BS-yxjA is to regulate gene expression through the binding of its aptamer region to guanine. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it causes changes in the riboswitch structure. Such structural changes lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. In the experiment, BS-yxjA demonstrated a high guanine binding affinity, which is consistent with its efficient transcriptional termination regulation ability in vivo. This highly efficient regulatory ability enables BS-yxjA to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby precisely regulating the expression levels of related genes.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-methoxy-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 9, is a chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one derivative featuring methoxy substituents at both the 9-position and on the 2-benzoyl group, and this compound with dual methoxy groups showed variable binding across different RNA motifs, being less selective than the top compounds but contributing to understanding functional group effects; the presence of methoxy groups typically aims to modulate the molecules electron density, metabolic stability, and polarity, this rigid structure, with multiple aromatic substituents, possesses several sites for hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions, suggesting potential for high-affinity binding to protein targets, and in reality, such complex fused heterocycles are often investigated as kinase inhibitors or receptor ligands, particularly in anticancer and anti-inflammatory drug development, with activity being highly dependent on the precise position of these substituents.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: Contains an A bulge (a single adenine nucleotide that is not base-paired).This structure mimics a specific bulge present only in the Dicer processing site of the miR-18a precursor.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)phenyl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as the benzimidazole-based RNA-binding substance, is a probe that is based on a mono-benzimidazole scaffold (instead of bis-benzimidazole) but still includes the tert-butyl and azide groups, and its core structure is benzimidazole which is known to have a binding preference for RNA, it also features a methylphenylpiperazine moiety that is crucial for its RNA-binding capability, and the azide group tag facilitates immobilization onto microarrays via click chemistry; mono-benzimidazoles are often precursors for antiviral, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory drugs, the presence of the azide group dictates its real-world activity as a Click Chemistry probe, it is designed to identify the biological targets of this specific mono-benzimidazole derivative through subsequent labeling and target characterization; in terms of binding properties, this compound does not bind to DNA but exclusively binds to specific RNA bulge structures, its binding to target RNAs has moderate affinity, and it specifically inhibits the processing of miR-17, -18a, and -20a by Dicer thereby suppressing the maturation of these oncogenic miRNAs; regarding its biological function, in cells it inhibits the production of miR-18a which in turn leads to the derepression of the STK4 protein and induces apoptosis in prostate cancer cells, additionally its specific binding to pre-miR-18a in cells has been validated using Chem-CLIP (Chemical Cross-Linking and Isolation by Pull-down) technology.

RNA information: 5′UAG/3′A_C (SL I): The UAG/A_C motif is located within HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I. Identified in the Inforna analysis as a ligandable element, it was enriched in binding assays, indicating its potential as a structural determinant for small molecule recognition and functional viral RNA inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[3,5-bis(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 9, is a highly symmetrical polyamine compound built upon a terephthalamide (benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide) core, its notable characteristic is that each side chain phenyl ring bears two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) groups at positions 3 and 5, with two such substituted phenyl groups attached to the core, the entire molecule contains a total of four imidazolinyl groups, all of which are strong bases and can be protonated at physiological pH, making it a potent tetracationic system, this structural design, concentrating multiple positive charges on an aromatic scaffold, is a classic strategy for developing DNA minor groove binders, due to the NH groups of the imidazolinyl moieties serving as hydrogen bond donors, the molecule interacts with the minor groove of DNA through a combination of electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals interactions, such tetracationic molecules are studied in medicinal chemistry as antifungal, antiprotozoal (e.g., antileishmanial), and anticancer agents, and this compound served as a negative control in RNA binding and antiviral assays, it did not exhibit significant binding to the enriched RNA motifs identified in the combinatorial screening, in agreement, it failed to interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II and showed no measurable inhibition of viral replication in cellular assays, its inactivity in both in vitro and in-cell experiments validated the specificity of Compounds 7, 8, and 10 for targeting HCV RNA motifs, demonstrating that not all small molecules engage structured RNA with functional consequences.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3, is an ultra-long bis-head molecule based on the bis-benzimidazole-DNA binder, characterized by the inclusion of four repeating polyamide units as a flexible spacer arm between the two active units, while 2H-3 itself incorporates three propylamine spacers between Ht-N3 modules to increase flexibility and the potential to span larger RNA repeat distances, its added length enhances its ability to engage multiple binding sites resulting in improved activity in cell-based assays and it shows slightly better selectivity than earlier analogs, functionally it can restore FXTAS-associated splicing defects in model systems more robustly than Ht-N3 likely due to a closer alignment with RNA repeat spacing, though its scaffold flexibility still prevents maximal preorganization restricting affinity compared with optimized derivatives like 2H-5; this compounds further increased spacer length is intended for the systematic study of the influence of linker length on the spatial adaptability and binding affinity of multivalent DNA binders, a longer spacer allows the two benzimidazole heads to bind to more distantly separated DNA sites or maintain flexible mobility in more complex biological environments, therefore it is a key molecule for structure-activity relationship studies, potentially applicable in long-range DNA cross-linking or multi-target anti-cancer drugs.

RNA information: r(CUG)2 is an RNA motif found in the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). It forms a stable stem-loop structure with two CUG repeats and can specifically bind to muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, leading to its sequestration and the resulting misregulation of alternative splicing. This RNA-protein interaction contributes to the molecular pathology of DM1, and small molecules targeting r(CUG)2 can disrupt the complex and restore splicing defects in disease models.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-5, is an ultra-long flexible bis-head benzimidazole molecule with seven repeating polyamide units, 2H-5 employs five propylamine spacers which produce a distance between Ht-N3 units closely mimicking the natural periodicity of 5CGG/3GGC motifs in r(CGG)exp, this structural optimization endows it with the highest potency and selectivity within the series with minimal loss of activity in the presence of tRNA, it also has strong binding affinity to r(CGG)12 and is the most effective compound at restoring splicing defects in FXTAS cellular models without interfering with downstream ORF translation, making it the optimal candidate, and this compound represents a further increase in length from the six-unit analogue (N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), in drug development, this minor length increase is vital for determining the optimal physiological length of the linker arm, the design with this maximum length is intended to simulate or achieve the farthest effective binding distance between proteins or nucleic acids, and this molecules potential real-world activity is focused on highly specific and low-toxicity targeted anti-cancer drugs, acting by long-range cross-linking or chelation of biomacromolecules to disrupt cellular function.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 3-chloro-N-[2-[4-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]anilino]quinazolin-2-yl]phenyl]propanamide, also referred to as a12, is a quinazoline derivative that includes a 3-chloropropanamide group, suggesting its potential use as a covalent or irreversible enzyme inhibitor; while a12 is only confirmed to bind G-quadruplex sequences in vitro and its screening target hVEGF-A is closely associated with tumor angiogenesis, this compound merely serves as a member of the screening library for potential anti-tumor candidates with no further functional or disease-related validation, and is a candidate for targeted anticancer or anti-inflammatory drugs.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: trimethyl-[2-[[5-methyl-11-(2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl]azanium diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with a diiodide counterion. It features a trimethylazanium side chain at position 7 and a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituent at position 11. It combines the polyaromatic nature of benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline with the strong positive charge of two quaternary ammonium centers. This compound is studied for the development of drugs to combat drug resistance. Particularly in anti-infective applications, its cationic nature allows for strong binding to negatively charged components on the bacterial surface, thereby disrupting cell membrane integrity, positioning it as a potential antibiotic replacement.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(1H-indol-2-yl)ethenyl]-1-methylquinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 15, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-(1H-indol-2-yl)vinyl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and no substitution at the 4-position, and it is a quaternary ammonium dye based on the quinoline and indole structures linked by an ethenyl bridge; the 1-methylquinolin-1-ium moiety provides a positive charge and high conjugation, making it a foundational structure for fluorescent nucleic acid probes, and it has certain affinity for A10-RNA-Wt as shown by SPR assay, can enhance ADAM10 translation as revealed by dual-luciferase reporter assay, and has certain cytotoxicity toward related cells, while this compound is used in molecular biology for detection and quantification of nucleic acids (DNA/RNA), where its fluorescence is significantly enhanced upon binding to the target.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: The 5 '-UTR of Bacillus subtilis tenA contains a classic TPP ribose switch: the secondary structure predicted by its mfold is composed of P1-P5 stem rings forming TPP binding pockets, which was confirmed by in-line probing in vitro; When the concentration of TPP (or toxic analogue PTPP) in cells increases, the ligand binds to the ribose switch with high affinity, inducing the expression platform to fold into a transcription terminator, thereby closing the transcription of the entire tenA-tenI-goxB-thiS-thiG-thiF-yjbV operator. The proteins encoded by these genes are responsible for the synthesis and salvage pathways of thiamine precursors HMP-PP and Thz-P. Therefore, ribose switches maintain TPP homeostasis through negative feedback. Mutations such as G94A in PT-resistant strains weaken the stability of P1 stems, causing the ribose switch to lose its ligand-sensing ability and the operon to be continuously expressed, thereby revealing that the TPP ribose switch is the target of the antibacterial compound pyrithiamine.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-[(4-amino-2-methylpyrimidin-5-yl)methyl]-4-methyl-1,3-thiazol-3-ium-5-yl]ethyl phosphono hydrogen phosphate chloride, also referred to as Thiamine Pyrophosphate (TPP) or Cocarboxylase, is a phosphate ester salt containing pyrimidine and thiazolium structures that is formed by the condensation of two precursors, hydroxymethylpyrimidine (HMP) and thiazole (Thz), after pyrophosphorylation; its structure is based on the pyrimidine and thiazole parent skeletons: the pyrimidine ring is substituted at position 5 with a (thiazoliumyl)methyl group, and the thiazolium ring is substituted at position 5 with a phosphorylated ethanol group, and in terms of biological function, this compound is the IUPAC name for the active form of Vitamin B1, which is a vital coenzyme and cofactor that catalyzes glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and the cleavage of C-C bonds in the pentose phosphate pathway as well as numerous enzymatic reactions including those in cellular energy production (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase complex), and its concentration is sensed in real time by the TPP riboswitch on mRNA - at high concentrations, it triggers transcription termination or translation masking, turning off all genes related to synthesis, transport and recycling; at low concentrations, the inhibition is released, thereby maintaining TPP homeostasis in bacteria and fungi through negative feedback and becoming a target for the antibacterial drug pyrithiamine.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide,
