and (2R)-6-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[[(2R)-2-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]propanoyl]-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]hexanamide, but with a reduced number of repeating alanine-amide units, containing two repeating units linked to the lysine backbone, this reduced chain length is part of a study to investigate the effect of a medium-length linker arm on the activity of the multivalent DNA binder, seeking an optimal balance between length, flexibility, and target affinity, it remains a highly cationic DNA minor groove binder, with potential real-world activity as an anti-proliferative agent achieved by interfering with genetic information function, and 2H-K2NPr displayed weaker potency, making it one of the less effective lysine PTAs, its activity in cellular assays was not highlighted, and stability advantages were not noted, the compound showed that this linker length poorly aligns recognition modules with RNA targets, reducing overall efficiency, and it was not considered further as a lead candidate.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-([1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, also referred to as the previously synthesized quindoline derivative that served as the control compound in the study, is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with a diamine side chain; this control compound once exhibited very strong binding affinity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex (NRQ) but demonstrated a profound lack of selectivity as it also bound significantly to the NRQ mutant which cannot form a canonical G-quadruplex structure, and this non-selective binding profile, likely due to strong, non-specific electrostatic interactions from its cationic nature, underscores the major drawback of previous-generation G-quadruplex ligands, while this structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents and its real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-1-(oxan-4-ylmethyl)quinolin-2-one, also referred to as SMSM69, is a non-planar coumarin analog featuring a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused core, carrying a 1 charge, which is built upon the quinolin-2-one scaffold and has a distinctive feature of the oxan-4-ylmethyl (tetrahydropyran-4-ylmethyl) substituent at the N1 position, a six-membered saturated ether ring that introduces an oxygen atom acting as a hydrogen bond acceptor, a strategy frequently employed in drug design to balance lipophilicity and hydrophilicity aiming to optimize oral absorption and distribution, the molecule retains the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl pharmacophore at C3 and the permanently positively charged quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium group at C7, recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions verified by FP and SAR data, acting as a selective RNA ligand, and this combination of a cationic center and a polar ether side chain suggests that it may be designed to target specific membrane-associated proteins or as a component in anti-cancer therapy, leveraging the well-known anti-cancer potential of quinoline derivatives with enhanced pharmacokinetic properties.

RNA information: r(GCGC): This model RNA is a GC-rich hairpin with a stem composed of repeating 5'-GC/3'-CG base pairs. Its key functional role in the study was to act as a stringent control for determining the selectivity of the investigated small molecules. Due to its high thermodynamic stability, the stem was shortened to 8 base pairs to make its melting temperature comparable to the longer AU-rich hairpins. Compounds with true AU selectivity, such as molecule 2 and AU-azide, demonstrated very weak binding (Kd in the micromolar range) to r(GCGC), thereby highlighting their specificity and minimizing the potential for off-target interactions with highly abundant GC-rich regions in the transcriptome.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(5-phenylfuran-2-yl)benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a compound containing a mono-imidamide group and a furan-phenyl structure, with the formula C17H14N2O; it binds to both AU and GC base pairs with high affinity but lacks selectivity, also recognizing RNA internal loops in disease repeats, making it non-ideal for specific targeting. As a mono-cationic imidamide derivative, its biological activity is distinct from diamidines; it likely lacks the potent DNA minor groove binding of its dicationic counterparts. Such compounds are often researched as enzyme inhibitors. In real-world applications, mono-imidamidines are frequently developed as serine protease inhibitors (such as thrombin or trypsin) for potential use in anticoagulant or anti-inflammatory therapy.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[2-aminoethyl-[2-[[2-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)acetyl]amino]ethyl]amino]ethyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as the imidazole-added acridine-triazine conjugate, is a compound that integrates Acridine (DNA intercalation), Triazine (hydrogen bonding recognition), and an Imidazole functional group, and this imidazole-added acridine-triazine design binds and cleaves both (CUG) and (CCUG) repeats effectively, inhibits transcription moderately, and shows strong bioactivity in cellular and fly models, the imidazole ring, key to molecules like Histamine and Histidine, often serves as a site for proton shuttle and acid-base catalysis in biological systems, this polyfunctional design suggests the compound aims to be a molecule with multiple targeting capabilities, for example, acridine targets nucleic acids, while the imidazole ring may be designed to target specific metalloenzymes (like zinc finger proteins) or G-quadruplex structures, its activity is thus focused on novel anticancer agents or molecular tools for investigating complex enzyme reactions and nucleic acid structures. --

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-6, is an extremely ultra-long chain bis-head benzimidazole molecule, featuring an exceptionally long linker arm with eight repeating polyamide units, and 2H-6 extends the linker to six propylamine spacers, offering slightly greater separation than 2H-5, this represents one of the longest flexible spacers in the series, this extreme length is designed to explore the upper limit of flexible linker arms on the activity of bis-head molecules, despite the increased flexibility, it remains one of the most potent analogs, indicating excellent selectivity, under physiological conditions, the molecule is likely to adopt a highly flexible random coil conformation, and this flexibility may allow it to adapt quickly to different binding sites in a dynamic environment or be used to non-specifically aggregate large intracellular molecules, although its affinity is somewhat weaker than 2H-5, the minimal difference suggests that it can still align effectively with RNA repeat spacing, in functional assays, 2H-6 restores splicing defects comparably to 2H-5, underscoring that optimized spacing near five or six linkers provides the best structural match for targeting r(CGG)exp hairpins, and it could potentially serve as a high-capacity molecular scaffold or a probe for studying biomacromolecular dynamics in real-world applications.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-cyclopropyl-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and cyclopropyl structures, modified from initial active analogs; its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a cyclopropylcarboxamide group at C2; in terms of biological function, Thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamides are a new class of antiplasmodial agents (anti-malarial) with strong antiparasitic activity, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, exhibiting moderate affinity and lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₄ sequence is an intermediate-length repeat consisting of four triplets, forming a more stable stem–loop than the dimer but still shorter than pathogenic expansions. Structurally, it offers a limited number of binding sites for ligands, sometimes resulting in inconsistent stoichiometry. Its reduced size diminishes the cooperativity observed in longer repeats, and it can behave differently depending on ligand structure. Functionally, r(CUG)₄ serves as a bridge model between minimal repeats and pathological expansions, highlighting how the length of the repeat influences recognition dynamics. However, it cannot fully mimic the complex misfolded conformations that drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,11S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide is a complex, symmetrical dimeric peptidic analog. Its structure is based on a C12 diene-diamide (dodec-6-enediamide, E-configured double bond) core, with the 2 and 11 positions linked to two N-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)proline units, and the termini connected to (2S)-2-(3-aminopropylamino)-6-aminohexanoyl (a lysine derivative) groups. It incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds. This compound has an amphiphilic structure. The benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the two free amino groups (on the side chain and terminus of the lysine derivative) likely make it cationic at physiological pH. In terms of biological function, peptidic analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands or enzyme inhibitors; its cationic and hydrophobic nature may confer membrane permeability or the ability to bind negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA/RNA), and benzo[g]quinoline compounds, like their quinoline isomers, typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: 5'CNG/3'CNG refers to repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide), commonly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: r(GGGGCC)₈ is an RNA repeat sequence that forms a structured RNA motif capable of binding small molecules. It adopts both hairpin and G-quadruplex conformations and plays a pathogenic role in c9FTD/ALS by undergoing repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation and forming nuclear RNA foci. Small molecules targeting r(GGGGCC)₈ can inhibit these toxic events, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure includes the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium group at position 7, along with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituted chain at position 11. This structure specifically incorporates two morpholine groups into the molecule. The compound has practical potential in bio-probes and drug delivery vehicles. As a therapeutic agent, its cationic and polycyclic properties allow it to act as a DNA damaging agent or a chemotherapy sensitizer, enhancing the efficacy of existing chemotherapeutic drugs by binding to tumor cell DNA or inhibiting key proteins related to DNA metabolism.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the derivative with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core that has the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, has the 2-piperidin-1-ylethylamine side chain, combining the ethylamine spacer with the piperidine six-membered ring, and this compound exhibited a good affinity for the NRQ as well as variable but generally potent anti-proliferative activity across cell lines, Piperidine is a ubiquitous structural unit in pharmaceuticals, optimizing lipophilicity and basicity, Piperidine-substituted quinoline derivatives are explored for antimalarial, anti-HIV, and kinase inhibitor activities, and this compounds design aims to regulate its tissue distribution and receptor affinity through the piperidine side chain.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 1-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)-3,4-dihydro-2H-quinoline, also referred to as the compound showing weaker binding affinity with unreported inhibitory activity against Dicer processing, connects the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core at the 6-position via a methylene bridge to the nitrogen atom of 3,4-dihydro-2H-quinoline (also known as tetrahydroquinoline); tetrahydroquinoline is a rigid and lipophilic pharmacophore, and fusing these two nitrogen-containing bicyclic heterocycles results in a large molecule with multiple basic centers, while tetrahydroquinoline derivatives are extensively used in the development of psychoactive drugs such as monoamine oxidase inhibitors or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, making this compound highly likely to be a candidate for a CNS-active drug, potentially for treating conditions like depression or Alzheimers disease due to its structure favoring bloodbrain barrier penetration

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as Guanine (G), is the basic purine nucleobase and a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that also acts as a natural ligand for the xpt-pbuX riboswitch and binds to aptamer with high affinity; its N1, N2, O6, N7 and N9 positions interact closely with the RNA-binding pocket through seven hydrogen bonds, inducing aptamer to fold and stabilize the P1 stem structure, promoting the formation of transcription terminators on the downstream expression platform, thereby shutting down the expression of purine transport and metabolism genes such as xpt-pbuX, and achieving purine homeostasis feedback regulation, it is one of the four principal bases found in DNA and RNA, and a product of purine metabolism, forms three hydrogen bonds with Cytosine (C) and is crucial for genetic information transfer, while the base itself has no direct clinical use, it is a foundational molecule for studying gene expression, DNA repair, and cell cycle regulation, and is the core scaffold for many antiviral and anticancer drugs.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine, also referred to as the derivative with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline scaffold that bears a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a propane-1,3-diamine side chain at the 11-position, features the propane-1,3-diamine side chain with a primary amine (NH2) terminus; this derivative exhibits very strong binding affinity and highly potent, broad-spectrum anti-proliferative activity with IC values below 3 M in all six cancer cell lines including A375, and the primary amine serves as a hydrogen bond donor and coordination site, providing a different mode of interaction with DNA bases or protein residues, which is used to study the effect of amine functionality on the antiproliferative activity and metabolic stability of DNA binding agents.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4c-6, is a large, planar quaternary ammonium salt based on the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline core, which demonstrated binding affinity for the NRQ, exhibited variable anti-proliferative activity with efficacy in A375 cells but no effect in HeLa cells at relevant concentrations; quaternary salts are often potent DNA intercalators and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents due to their positive charge and ability to disrupt cell membranes, and this structure is typically investigated for its strong anticancer activity.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-2-yl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzamide, also referred to as Compound 18 which is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative and a regioisomer with a 2-benzoic amide moiety, is a positional isomer where the benzamide group is attached to the 2-amino group of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine ring, leaving the 3-position with a free amino group, and it also carries the 3-(trifluoromethyl)benzoyl group; this compound binds HIV-1 TAR and RRE RNAs, key elements for viral replication, and as a potential RNA-binding ligand, it targets HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, though with moderate affinity compared to other analogs, meanwhile the study of such amide-position isomers is crucial in drug discovery, as small changes in linkage position can lead to significant differences in biological activity, metabolic stability, and toxicity, and this molecule is also categorized as a candidate for a kinase inhibitor, researched for its potential anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory effects.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a typical dicationic structure, consisting of the azonium on the pentacyclic scaffold and the azonium on the 3-methylpyridinium ring. This combination of dual positive charges and a highly lipophilic large scaffold makes it a potent cell membrane permeabilizer. Pyridinium rings are common cationic units. In practical applications, such amphiphilic, highly charged compounds are widely explored for disrupting the integrity of biological membranes, thus exhibiting potent, broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against both bacteria and fungi. It may also be studied as a cellular tracer or fluorescent probe for bioimaging.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[(E)-2-(3-fluorophenyl)ethenyl]cinnoline, also referred to as CP10, is a compound based on the cinnoline core, connected to a 3-fluorophenyl group via a trans-ethenyl linker (i.e., a trans double bond, with its structure featuring a naphthalene-fused heterocycle linked to the 3-fluorophenyl ring through this trans double bond), with the formula C16H11FN2; the molecule possesses a rigid conjugated system structurally related to stilbene, though its conjugated system and fluorine atom were not sufficient to confer strong, specific binding as reflected in its poor binding parameters, and cinnoline derivatives are associated with kinase inhibition, this type of compound is explored as a receptor ligand or signaling pathway modulator, potentially exhibiting anti-inflammatory, anticancer, or utility in the study of cardiovascular diseases through its interaction with biological targets via its extended conjugation.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[4-[(2-amino-5-methyl-4-oxo-3,6,7,8-tetrahydropteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as 5-methyl THF (5-methyltetrahydrofolate), is a non-azaaromatic folate derivative with a pteridine core (reduced, lacking planarity), a p-aminobenzoyl group, a glutamate chain, and a methyl group at the 5-position, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis and whose key role is as a methyl donor in biological methylation reactions unrelated to azaaromatic sensing, this compound is an analog of Folic Acid, structurally related to the crucial anticancer drug Methotrexate (a DHFR inhibitor), and is likely a metabolite or a potential antimetabolite anti-tumor agent.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: actinium;2-amino-3-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-N-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]phenyl]propanamide, also referred to as N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-histidinamide (3b), is a compound whose organic part is a peptidomimetic derived from Histidine, featuring the 2-amino-3-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)propanoyl backbone, and N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-histidinamide (3b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate designed to mimic the natural Tat peptide and compete for binding to the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulgeloop, the imidazole ring of Histidine is a key pharmacophore that can be protonated at physiological pH, lending it hydrophilicity and the ability to act as a coordinating ligand, this organic moiety is linked to the 3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]aniline substituent, it replaces the labile ribose-phosphate backbone with a rigid thiazolearyl core that presents an imidazole-functionalized histidine side-chain for hydrogen-bonding and -stacking within the TAR bulge, crucially, the compounds name is prefixed with actinium, a radioactive element, particularly Actinium-225, an important alpha-particle emitting radioisotope, therefore, the compounds true function is as a radiopharmaceutical: the complex organic structure serves as a chelator or targeting ligand, responsible for delivering the radioactive Actinium atom to a specific biological target, such as a cancer cell receptor, for Targeted Alpha Therapy (TAT), in vitro, 3b binds TAR with an affinity comparable to neomycin, triggers no global RNA conformational change, and retains high specificity against excess tRNA or dsDNA, in HIV-infected MAGIC-5B reporter cells, 3b blocks Tat-mediated trans-activation with notable potencyequipotent to a reference concentration of AZTand shows no cytotoxicity up to a high tested concentration, these properties establish 3b as a metabolically stable, highly specific small-molecule surrogate of Tat that selectively silences the TAR riboswitch and aborts HIV transcription. HIV

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-7, is an ultra-long flexible bis-head benzimidazole molecule with the identical chemical name and structural description as N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methyl]amino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, featuring a linker arm with eight repeating polyamide units, and 2H-7 incorporates seven propylamine spacers, creating the longest intermodule distance tested, it represents the maximum length among the flexible linker arms in this study, emphasizing its role as a highly flexible, multivalent DNA binder or biomacromolecular cross-linker, this excess length reduces preorganization and likely misaligns the RNA-binding modules with r(CGG)exp repeats, consequently, it demonstrates reduced potency compared to 2H-5 and 2H-6, its predicted real-world activity remains nucleic acid targeting for anti-proliferative or gene regulation purposes, likely leveraging its long reach and flexibility, while it still shows some functional rescue of splicing defects, its diminished efficiency highlights the importance of matching linker length to RNA motif periodicity, and the results from 2H-7 reinforce that optimal potency arises from precise spatial organization, with excessive linker extension leading to loss of affinity and selectivity.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 1-cyclopropyl-7-(4-ethylpiperazin-1-yl)-6-fluoro-4-oxoquinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Enrofloxacin, is a classic Fluoroquinolone antibiotic with N1-cyclopropyl, C6-fluoro, and C7-piperazine substituents (similar to Ciprofloxacin) that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, and is used clinically as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial for infections including those of the respiratory and urinary tracts.

RNA information: cTNT (cardiac troponin T) pre-mRNA is a splicing reporter RNA that contains an alternatively spliced exon 5. It is regulated by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, which binds upstream of exon 5 and represses its inclusion. In myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA sequesters MBNL1, leading to increased exon 5 inclusion in cTNT mRNA. Thus, cTNT splicing serves as a functional readout for MBNL1 activity and DM1-associated splicing defects in cellular models .
Small_molecule_information: 6-nitro-2-[3-(6-nitro-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)propyl]-1H-benzimidazole, also referred to as Benzimidazole 1 (2015), is a symmetric or near-symmetric molecule composed of two benzimidazole units connected by a flexible three-carbon (propyl) chain, with each benzimidazole ring carrying a nitro group at the 6-position, and Benzimidazole 1 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative optimized for selective recognition of the pathogenic r(CUG)109 RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole scaffold, fused from a benzene ring and an imidazole ring, is a core structure in many pharmaceuticals (such as antifungals and anthelmintics), the two benzimidazole units are linked by a flexible alkyl chain, allowing the molecule to bind to biological targets in various conformations, while the nitro groups enhance the molecules electron-withdrawing capacity and potential redox activity, in real-world applications, nitro-containing benzimidazole derivatives are frequently investigated as antifungals, antiparasitics (e.g., antitrypanosomal or antimalarial agents), or anticancer drugs, with mechanisms possibly related to interfering with microbial or cancer cell DNA replication, or acting as substrates for bioreduction, by mimicking key hydrogen-bond and -stacking contacts normally supplied by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, it binds the periodic 11 UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable affinity in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from the toxic transcript, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering the transcript level of DMPK mRNA or affecting off-target RNAs such as the human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like profilemoderate molecular weight (398 Da), cLogP 3.4, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mmakes Benzimidazole 1 a validated chemical probe for demonstrating that non-charged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs implicated in human disease. RNA

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic compound based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium core with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure results from the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium quaternary ammonium group at position 7. The morpholine group, a common medicinal scaffold, influences the molecules hydrophilicity and targeting. This compound is researched for antiviral and anticancer applications. Its polycyclic planar structure and cationic charge allow it to interact with viral nucleic acids or host cell DNA, thereby interfering with viral replication or cellular proliferation.

RNA information: RNA5 contains the GG internal loop for compound 2 binding, but the UU loop for compound 1 has been mutated to a UA base pair. This mutation severely diminishes binding by dimeric compound 3, demonstrating that the UU loop is essential for its high-affinity, bivalent interaction.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[[(2S)-2-[[(2R)-1-[(E,2R,7R)-8-[(2R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-azaniumylpropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2,7-bis[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-8-oxooct-4-enoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]propylazanium, also referred to as Compound 3E, is a complex peptidomimetic that is an olefin-linked analog derived from an earlier disulfide-bridged lead, retains two heteroaromatic intercalating units essential for binding the HIV-1 FSS RNA upper stem-loop, has moderate binding affinity and shows sequence selectivity over nonspecific RNAs but did not demonstrate antiviral activity in pseudotyped HIV assays which motivated the structural optimization that led to incorporation of larger benzo[g]quinoline groups in later analogs, and this compound differs from 3-[[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-[(E,2S,7S)-8-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-azaniumylpropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2,7-bis[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-8-oxooct-4-enoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]propylazanium in two major ways: the core and proline residues are of non-natural (2R,7R) and (2R) chirality, making it an enantiomer or diastereomer of related compounds; and the highly hydrophobic 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group is simplified to the smaller, less lipophilic 2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl group, the inversion of chirality means that this compound is likely to exhibit completely different biological activities and potentially higher aqueous solubility due to the smaller aromatic group, and it may be designed to target receptors or enzymes that are inaccessible or poorly bound by the S-enantiomer.

RNA information: BS-xpt(A73G) is a mutant of Bacillus subtilis BS-xpt guanine riboswitch, with A at position 73 (aptamer core, near ligand-binding site) mutated to G. Structurally, this mutation disrupts the wild-type core folding. Functionally, it abolishes 2AP binding (unlike natural G73-containing aptamers) and impairs guanine binding, potentially reducing its in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency compared to wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

Small_molecule_information: 3-chloro-N-[2-[4-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]anilino]quinazolin-2-yl]phenyl]propanamide, also referred to as a12, is a quinazoline derivative that includes a 3-chloropropanamide group, suggesting its potential use as a covalent or irreversible enzyme inhibitor; while a12 is only confirmed to bind G-quadruplex sequences in vitro and its screening target hVEGF-A is closely associated with tumor angiogenesis, this compound merely serves as a member of the screening library for potential anti-tumor candidates with no further functional or disease-related validation, and is a candidate for targeted anticancer or anti-inflammatory drugs.

Small_molecule_information: 16-[3-(3-fluoropyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a fluorinated analog of 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.02,10.04,8.015,20]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene, with a fluorine atom introduced at the 3-position of the pyridinium ring. The introduction of fluorine is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry to optimize lead compounds, as fluorine can significantly affect a molecules lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and electronegativity, thereby fine-tuning its interaction with targets. 16-[3-(3-fluoropyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.02,10.04,8.015,20]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene remains a dicationic compound, maintaining a strong propensity for membrane activity. Functionally, it is expected to retain the antimicrobial properties of its dicationic parent core, but the introduction of fluorine may improve its pharmacokinetic (ADME) properties, such as enhanced bioavailability and stability in vivo.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-7-fluoro-2-methyl-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-10-oxo-4-oxa-1-azatricyclo[7.3.1.05,13]trideca-5(13),6,8,11-tetraene-11-carboxylic acid is a derivative of the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics. Its structure, featuring a fluorine atom at position 7, a carboxylic acid group, and a piperazine ring, is characteristic of broad-spectrum synthetic antibacterial agents. These drugs function by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and Topoisomerase IV, thereby blocking bacterial DNA replication and repair. This compound is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug candidate used for treating complex infections caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-phenylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 16 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with the piperazines secondary amine modified where Rphenyl and synthesized via one-step coupling that binds the influenza A RNA promoter and has improved anti-influenza activity), has an acyl side chain that is the benzoyl group (phenylmethanone); the introduction of the aromatic benzoyl group significantly increases lipophilicity and provides a large hydrophobic surface area for interaction with biological targets, while the core activity remains 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, and the benzoyl substitution is often found in compounds with anti-anxiety or antipsychotic potential, thus it might be studied for treating hypertension complicated by anxiety or depression due to its potential affinity for certain CNS receptors.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2R,11R)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 10 (2012), is a structurally massive cis (Z) peptidomimetic dimer that incorporates an extended hydrocarbon bridge via pentenyl glycine residues, producing greater linker length and conformational flexibility which allows the benzo[g]quinoline groups to adopt more favorable orientations for engaging RNA repeats, and this structural extension enhances the ability of the compound to match the geometry of expanded repeat hairpins; functionally, it exhibits strong activity in cellular models, efficiently penetrating cells and rescuing functional phenotypes caused by toxic RNA, and it demonstrates that tuning linker length and flexibility can significantly improve recognition efficiency and biological performance, making it a promising scaffold for therapeutic exploration, while its Z-configuration of the core twelve-carbon chain forces a rigid U-shaped bend in the main chain, significantly altering its overall spatial conformation, and this conformational change is critical for the molecules biological activity as it determines the relative distance and orientation of the two active units (benzo[g]quinoline/lysine side chains) in space, and this specific bent conformation may make it more suitable for binding to DNA regions with a particular curvature or to oligomeric protein receptors, and the molecule maintains the powerful polycationic character and the DNA binding potential of the benzo[g]quinoline groups, thus this compound is designed as a crucial molecule for studying the conformation-activity relationship of large polycationic peptidomimetics, with potential applications as a highly specific anti-cancer agent or gene therapy vector.
